narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a middle-income family background and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put significant effort into your undergraduate studies and LSAT preparation, but I know receiving this rejection letter must feel disheartening. The admissions model we use predicted a 96% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application for our law school program was unfortunately denied—our goal is to select students with the strongest likelihood of bar success to ensure everyone thrives. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic rankings in school, particularly your position in the lowest decile for grades in Year 3, which had the strongest influence in pushing the model toward expecting a bar failure. Your LSAT score of 25 also played a major role, significantly contributing to that concerning prediction in a similar way. Even your Year 1 decile ranking in the lowest group added to this risk signal, reinforcing the model's worries about your bar exam readiness. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.70 actually worked in your favor, nudging the prediction slightly toward expecting you to pass, unlike many applicants where lower GPAs hurt more. Your full-time enrollment plan helped a bit too, as most successful students share that status, providing a small counterbalance. Compared to typical students—who average much higher deciles around the 5th or 6th for both years, LSAT scores near 37, and GPAs over 3.2—your profile stands out as lower in these key academic areas, which is why the model flagged higher failure risk for you specifically. Your middle-income background had minimal sway either way. While this outcome stings, it's not the end of your journey; consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your undergrad record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and seeking targeted bar prep strategies early. Many students in similar situations have turned things around by addressing these exact gaps—reach out to admissions counselors for personalized advice on reapplying stronger next cycle. You've got the determination; now focus on those academic standouts to shift the odds in your favor. We're rooting for your success.
